methodology: Conservative de-duplication: only clusters WINs that draw on the same primary dataset or where one WIN's value is derived from another's data. Does NOT cluster by topic similarity alone — only by data-source overlap, restatement, or mathematical dependency.

clusters.name: Schumann / Tesla cavity resonance
clusters.shared_data: Earth-ionosphere cavity electromagnetic resonance (7.83 Hz observation + Tesla's 0.08484 s propagation time)
clusters.why_not_independent: WIN-001's 11.78 Hz and WIN-062's 1.574c are the same Tesla measurement expressed as frequency vs speed. WIN-002/029/038/061 all concern the 7.83 Hz Schumann resonance — four descriptions of one observation.

clusters.name: Tidal harmonic constituents
clusters.shared_data: Tide gauge harmonic decomposition (Doodson 1921 equilibrium theory)
clusters.why_not_independent: Five frequencies from a single tidal spectrum. All predicted by standard equilibrium tidal theory. The dome does not derive any constituent frequency from its geometry — it adopts the standard values and counts each as a separate WIN.

clusters.name: SAA spatial morphology & drift
clusters.shared_data: INTERMAGNET magnetometer network measurements of SAA shape, position, and evolution
clusters.why_not_independent: Six descriptions of the same geomagnetic feature (SAA splitting, cell positions, decay rates) from the same station network. All are explained by MHD core dynamics (Terra-Nova et al. 2017).

clusters.name: NMP position & trajectory
clusters.shared_data: NOAA/WMM North Magnetic Pole tracking data
clusters.why_not_independent: Six descriptions of the same pole trajectory: pre-1990 speed, post-1990 acceleration, 1990 inflection, angular deviation, longitudinal ratio, and recent deceleration. All from the same position time series.

clusters.name: Eclipse gravity / coupling
clusters.shared_data: Superconducting gravimeter data during solar eclipses
clusters.why_not_independent: WIN-012's coupling constant κ=1.67 is derived from WIN-011's Mohe data (not independent). WIN-013/014 are null results from other stations for the same eclipse gravity claim.

clusters.name: Geomagnetic field decay rate
clusters.why_not_independent: Three claims about the same trend: field decay threshold (≥28 nT), annual rate (≥28 nT/year), and hemisphere asymmetry ratio. All from the same time series.

clusters.name: Eclipse magnetometer response
clusters.why_not_independent: Same prediction (magnetic dip during eclipse) applied to two events. WIN-025 extends WIN-010 from 1 station to 9 stations but tests the identical claim.

clusters.name: Telluric EM frequency
clusters.why_not_independent: Two claims about the same frequency spectrum: a cutoff at 11.7 Hz and a peak near 12 Hz.

clusters.name: Stellar astrometry
clusters.why_not_independent: Aberration and parallax are two components of the same astrometric measurement pipeline. Both are refuted by the same Gaia DR3 dataset.

clusters.name: Analemma solar geometry
clusters.shared_data: Solar analemma observations (sun position throughout the year)
clusters.why_not_independent: Day-length variation and loop ratio are two properties of the same figure-8 solar track. Both use the 23.44° obliquity the dome borrows from globe geometry.

counter_argument: The dome could argue that each WIN represents a numerically distinct prediction: M2's 12.42-hour period is a different number from S2's 12.00-hour period, so they're separate verifications. This has surface validity — you can't derive M2 from S2 without additional physics. But the issue isn't whether the numbers are distinct; it's whether the dome model derives any of them from its geometry. All five tidal frequencies are adopted from standard equilibrium tidal theory (Doodson 1921), verified against the same tide-gauge data. The dome adds zero predictive content to any of them. Counting each separately inflates the score without adding independent evidence for the dome model. The same applies to NMP trajectory WINs: saying 'the pole was here in 1990,' 'the pole accelerated after 1990,' and 'the pole is 18° from 120E' are three descriptions of the same time series, not three independent predictions.
replacement_html:
De-Duplication Analysis: 67 WINs → {{INDEPENDENT_CLAIMS}} Independent Claims (click to expand)

The dome model counts 67 “confirmed predictions.” But many of these are multiple descriptions of the same observation, multiple readings from the same dataset, or values mathematically derived from each other. A conservative de-duplication — clustering only WINs that share primary data sources or where one WIN’s value is derived from another’s — reduces the count significantly.

Methodology: Two WINs are clustered only if they (a) draw on the same measurement dataset, (b) one is mathematically derived from the other, or (c) they describe different properties of the same observation. We do not cluster by topic similarity alone — WIN-053 (two-pole geomagnetic model) and the SAA cluster both involve geomagnetic data, but WIN-053 is a model-fitting exercise on the full IGRF dataset, not an SAA observation.

ClusterWINsCountShared Data Source
Schumann / Tesla cavity resonance001, 002, 029, 038, 061, 0626 → 1Earth-ionosphere cavity EM resonance. WIN-001’s 11.78 Hz and WIN-062’s 1.574c are the same Tesla measurement (0.08484 s) expressed as frequency vs. speed. WIN-002/029/038/061 all concern the 7.83 Hz Schumann resonance.
Tidal harmonic constituents045, 046, 049, 050, 0515 → 1Five frequencies from one tide-gauge FFT. All predicted by standard equilibrium tidal theory (Doodson 1921). None derived from dome geometry.
SAA spatial morphology & drift004, 005, 035, 040, 041, 0606 → 1Six descriptions of the SAA’s shape, drift, and decay from the same INTERMAGNET station network.
NMP position & trajectory006, 007, 022, 036, 043, 0596 → 1Six descriptions of the North Magnetic Pole’s position and velocity from the same NOAA/WMM tracking data.
Eclipse gravity / coupling011, 012, 013, 0144 → 1WIN-012’s κ=1.67 is derived from WIN-011’s Mohe data (not independent). WIN-013/014 are null results from other stations for the same claim.
Geomagnetic field decay rate037, 042, 0633 → 1Three claims about the same IGRF trend: decay threshold (≥28 nT), annual rate (≥28 nT/year), and hemisphere asymmetry ratio.
Eclipse magnetometer response010, 0252 → 1Same prediction (magnetic dip during eclipse) applied to 2017 (1 station) and 2024 (9 stations).
Telluric EM frequency008, 0092 → 1Two claims about the same frequency spectrum: a cutoff at 11.7 Hz and a peak near 12 Hz.
Stellar astrometry016, 0172 → 1Aberration and parallax are two components of the same Gaia/Hipparcos positional measurement.
Analemma solar geometry018, 0192 → 1Day-length variation and loop ratio are two properties of the same figure-8 solar track.
Unclustered29 WINs → 29 claimsEach draws on a distinct dataset or makes a claim not derivable from any other WIN.
Total: 67 WINs → {{INDEPENDENT_CLAIMS}} independent claims ({{DEDUP_REDUCTION_PERCENT}}% reduction). Even the {{INDEPENDENT_CLAIMS}} surviving claims are subject to the individual verdict analysis above — de-duplication only addresses count inflation, not claim validity.

What this means: When the dome model reports “67 confirmed predictions,” 38 of those are re-descriptions of 10 observations. The tidal cluster is the clearest example: five harmonic constituents (M2, S2, K1, O1, N2) are read from a single tide-gauge Fourier transform. All five are predicted by standard equilibrium tidal theory — a framework developed by Doodson in 1921. The dome model does not derive any tidal frequency from its geometry; it adopts the standard values and counts each as a separate WIN. Similarly, the NMP cluster counts six WINs for the trajectory of a single pole: its pre-1990 speed, post-1990 acceleration, 1990 inflection point, angular deviation, longitudinal ratio, and recent deceleration — six descriptions of one time series.

A fair objection: The dome could argue that M2’s 12.42-hour period is a different number from S2’s 12.00-hour period, so each is a separate verification. This is true — you cannot derive M2 from S2 without additional physics. But the issue is not whether the numbers are distinct; it is whether the dome derives any of them from dome geometry. None of the tidal WINs trace their predicted values to dome parameters (disc_radius, firmament_height, etc.). They are adopted wholesale from standard physics and counted individually. This inflates the score without adding independent evidence for the dome model.

Note: This analysis uses conservative criteria. Only WINs sharing primary data sources are clustered. A less conservative approach — also clustering by topic overlap (e.g., WIN-024 Roaring 40s with the SAA cluster, WIN-039 lunar magnetic with the tidal cluster) — would reduce the count further.

placeholder_tokens.INDEPENDENT_CLAIMS: Computed from wins.json at build time: 67 minus sum of (cluster_size - 1) for each cluster. Currently 39.

css_needed: .dedup-table summary { cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.1em; padding: 8px 0; } .dedup-table table.dedup { width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; margin: 12px 0; font-size: 0.95em; } .dedup-table table.dedup th, .dedup-table table.dedup td { border: 1px solid #ddd; padding: 8px; vertical-align: top; } .dedup-table table.dedup thead { background: #f5f5f5; } .dedup-table table.dedup tr.dedup-total { background: #f9f9f9; font-weight: bold; } .dedup-table table.dedup tfoot { background: #f0f0f0; }
build_integration_notes: The {{INDEPENDENT_CLAIMS}} and {{DEDUP_REDUCTION_PERCENT}} placeholders need to be added to the replacement map in generate-html.js. The cluster definitions should be computed from wins.json (matching detail_group fields + the additional clusters defined here) so the count auto-updates if WINs are added or reclassified. Alternatively, hardcode 39 for now and add a TODO to compute it.
anticipated_objections: Each WIN has a different numerical value → addressed in 'fair objection' paragraph
WIN-025 (2024 eclipse) is genuinely new data compared to WIN-010 (2017) → acknowledged but same prediction methodology, same claim being tested
Clustering is subjective → methodology section explains the conservative criteria
Some unclustered WINs might also overlap (e.g., WIN-024 with SAA) → noted in closing caveat
kernel_of_truth: The dome model's 67-WIN count has surface validity because each WIN cites a different numerical value. The counting method is not fraudulent — it's how the model's monitoring infrastructure works (one domain per WIN). But the scientific question is independent predictive content, not numerical distinctness. By that measure, 38 of the 67 WINs are restatements of 10 observations.
